---
title: "Winning a 3-Minute Talk"
subtitle: "Structure, Story, and Impact"
author: "Dr. Thiyanga S. Talagala<br>Department of Statistics, University of Sri Jayewardenepura"
format:
  revealjs: 
    theme: [default, styles.scss]
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    slide-number: true
    title-slide-attributes: 
      data-background-color: "#800026"
      data-background-image: none
---

::: {.fragment .big-text}
Why a 3-minute talk?
:::

::: {.fragment .big-text}
Who invented it?
:::

::: {.fragment .big-text}
What was the purpose?
:::



# Why a 3-minute talk?


::: {.fragment .medium-text}

Many researchers can explain the <span style="color:blue;">details</span>. clearly, but struggle to communicate the <span style="color:red;">big picture</span>.

:::

#

::: columns

::: column
## Big Picture

- Elephant satellite neck belts (or GPS collars) are heavy-duty tracking devices worn around the neck to monitor elephant movements.

- Using elephant movement data to guide conservation actions and reduce risk to wildlife during extreme climate events like El Niño.

:::

::: column

## Details

- Statistics  
- Models  
- Trajectory Analysis  
- Spatial Analysis  
:::

:::

# Who invented it?

Universities noticed:

  - Research was becoming too technical
  - Public impact was weak
  - Even other academics struggled to understand work outside their field
  

::: {.fragment .small-text}
<span style="color:blue;"> The 3-minute talk was created as a **research communication training tool** at the University of Queensland (2008) to help researchers explain complex ideas clearly, quickly, and to non-specialists.</span>
:::

# What was the purpose?

1. **Improve research communication** by helping researchers explain complex ideas clearly to non-experts.

2. **Improve clarity of thinking** by forcing researchers to focus on the core idea, not just technical details.

3. **Bridge academia and the public** by making research more accessible, understandable, and impactful for society.

# First 15 Seconds Matter

- Research shows audiences decide whether to listen within seconds.


::: {.fragment}

 - Avoid:
 
    "Good morning everyone..."
  
    "Today I am going to talk about..."


:::

::: {.fragment .medium-text}


Instead, start with a hook.

:::

# What is a Hook?

A hook is used at the beginning of a presentation to:

1. Capture Attention

People decide very quickly whether they want to listen. A strong hook grabs their attention from the start.

2. Create Interest

It makes the audience curious about your topic and encourages them to hear more.

3. Introduce the Problem

A hook often leads naturally into the issue or challenge you are addressing.

4. Establish Relevance

It helps the audience understand why the topic matters to them or to society.

5. Set the Tone

The hook establishes the mood of your presentation—serious, inspiring, surprising, or thought-provoking.



## Hook


1. Start with a Surprising Statistic

    Every year, millions of tons of plastic waste enter our oceans.
    
2. Ask a Question
    
    What if a single manufacturing defect could cause an entire production line to fail? 
    

3. Show an object or image.
 
## Managing the 3 Minutes


Problem: 1 min

Solution: 1 min

Impact: 1 min


## Tips

Capturing attention quickly

Explaining a problem clearly

Convincing the audience that the solution matters

## What should the audience remember after 3 minutes?

A strong 3MT is not about saying everything.
It is about making the audience understand one important idea clearly and remember it.

The audience should remember:

    - One problem

    - One solution

    - One message
    
## UQ Rules

 - Presentations are limited to 3 minutes maximum and competitors exceeding 3 minutes are disqualified.

  - Presentations are to be spoken word (e.g. no poems, raps or songs).

  - Presentations are considered to have commenced when a presenter starts their presentation through either movement or speech.

 - A single static PowerPoint slide is permitted. No slide transitions, animations or movement) and is to be presented from the beginning of the oration.

  - No additional electronic media (e.g. sound and video files) are permitted.

 - No additional props (e.g. costumes, musical instruments, laboratory equipment) are permitted.

 - The decision of the adjudicating panel is final.
 
source: [https://threeminutethesis.uq.edu.au/resources/competition-rules](https://threeminutethesis.uq.edu.au/resources/competition-rules)

## Revise and Practice

A great 3-minute talk is built through rehearsal.

  - Read your talk aloud multiple times

  - Read your script aloud (not silently)
  
  - Record yourself and listen critically

  - Time yourself strictly—3 minutes is short
  
  - Practice with people outside your field
    
    Ask: “Did they understand the main idea?”
    
## Feedback Questions to Ask

  - Was my message clear?

  - Did I sound engaging?

  - Was anything confusing or too technical?

  - Did I finish within 3 minutes?
  
## Deliver with Energy and Engagement

  - Speak in an enthusiastic and confident tone

  - Show genuine interest in your topic

  - Your energy helps the audience stay engaged
  
## Word Choice (What you say)

Use language that is:

  - Simple

  - Direct

  - Easy to understand

## Good practices

 - Use short sentences (10–20 words)

 - Prefer active voice

 - Avoid unnecessary jargon
 
## Language to Avoid

  - “Obviously…” (can sound dismissive)

  - Overly formal academic connectors (therefore, hence, thus)

  - Heavy jargon without explanation

  - Long, complex sentences

  - Idioms that may confuse non-native speakers
  
# Words and Phrases to Avoid in Spoken Presentations

## Formal academic connectors

These make speech sound written and unnatural:

Therefore

Hence

Thus

Moreover

Furthermore

Consequently

In conclusion (often overused in short talks)

# Overly academic or rigid phrasing

These sound like journal writing, not speaking:

“It can be observed that…”

“It is evident that…”

“As previously mentioned…”

“The aforementioned…”

“In the present study…”

## Dismissive or alienating words

These can make the audience feel excluded or “talked down to”:

 - Obviously

- Clearly (when used to assume agreement)

- As you all know

- In order to

## Filler academic habits

These interrupt natural flow:

 - “Basically…”

 - “In fact…” 

 - “Actually…” 

 - “So basically what I am trying to say is…”
 
## Instead of formal connectors, use:

Short pauses

Simple transitions like:

  “So…”
  
  “This means…”
  
  “What this shows is…”
  
  “Now the problem is…”
  
## Written Text OR Oral Presentation Style ?

 - Oral communication presents many benefits.

 - It is a quick and direct method of communication.	

 - Be it criticism, praise, or information, oral communication conveys the message immediately to the receiver.	

 - Moreover, oral communication elicits immediate feedback and hence enables a two-way communication.	

# Oral Presentation Style


 - Oral communication presents many benefits.	<span style="color:red;"> Oral communication has many benefits.</span>

- It is a quick and direct method of communication.	<span style="color:red;"> It is quick and direct.</span>

 - Be it criticism, praise, or information, oral communication conveys the message immediately to the receiver.	<span style="color:red;"> Whether we are criticizing, praising, or sharing information, we can deliver the message immediately.</span>

- Moreover, oral communication elicits immediate feedback and hence enables a two-way communication.	<span style="color:red;"> Also, we get immediate feedback, so it becomes a two-way communication.</span>


# Your Turn


- Plastics are a type of polymer widely used in daily life.	

- They are lightweight and durable materials.

- Because of these properties, plastics are used in packaging, bottles, and containers.

- However, plastics can cause environmental pollution if not managed properly.	


# Help

- Plastics are a type of polymer widely used in daily life.		<span style="color:red;"> Plastics are a common type of polymer we use every day.</span>

- They are lightweight and durable materials.		<span style="color:red;">They are light and strong materials.</span>

- Because of these properties, plastics are used in packaging, bottles, and containers.		<span style="color:red;"> That’s why we use them in packaging, bottles, and containers.</span>

- However, plastics can cause environmental pollution if not managed properly.		<span style="color:red;"> But if we don’t manage them well, they can cause environmental pollution.</span>

# Your Turn


 - Polymers are large molecules composed of repeating structural units called monomers.	

 - These monomers are chemically bonded together to form long chains.	

 - The properties of a polymer depend on its molecular structure and arrangement.

 - Therefore, by modifying the structure, we can tailor materials for specific applications.	


# Help


Polymers are large molecules composed of repeating structural units called monomers.	<span style="color:red;">Polymers are large molecules made up of repeating units called monomers.</span>

These monomers are chemically bonded together to form long chains.	<span style="color:red;">These monomers join together to form long chains.</span>

The properties of a polymer depend on its molecular structure and arrangement.<span style="color:red;">	A polymer’s properties depend on its structure and how the chains are arranged.</span>

Therefore, by modifying the structure, we can tailor materials for specific applications.	<span style="color:red;"> So by changing the structure, we can design materials for different uses.</span>

# Watch Videos of Good Speakers

 - What do you notice about the speaker’s delivery?

 - How does the speaker use pace, tone, movement and body language?

- What do they do with their hands?

# Articulation Practice

Open your mouth fully and exaggerate the sounds:

Laaaaaa

Leeeeee

Liiiiii

Loooooo

Luuuuuu

# Projection Exercise

Imagine speaking to:

Someone 1 meter away

Someone at the back of the room

Someone in the last row of an auditorium

# Speaker Warm-Up

Red leather, yellow leather.

Unique New York.

Big black bugs bleed blue black blood.

Toy boat, toy boat, toy boat.

Clear Consonants

# Repeat slowly, then faster:

Pa Pa Pa Pa Pa

Ba Ba Ba Ba Ba

Ta Ta Ta Ta Ta

Da Da Da Da Da

Ka Ka Ka Ka Ka

Ga Ga Ga Ga Ga

# Slide Design for 3-Minute Talks: Using Gestalt Principles

Why Gestalt Principles?

The audience sees your slide for only a few seconds before deciding:

What is important?

What belongs together?

## Gestalt Laws

The Gestalt Laws (or Gestalt Principles) are a set of principles from psychology that explain how people naturally organize and interpret visual information. Rather than seeing individual elements separately, people tend to perceive them as meaningful groups, patterns, or wholes.

Reading: [https://daydreamingnumbers.com/gestalt-laws-data-visualization/](https://daydreamingnumbers.com/gestalt-laws-data-visualization/)

## Why is it important for a 3-Minute Presentation?

The audience has only a few seconds to understand your slide. Gestalt principles help them:

 - Find the important message quickly

 - Understand relationships between ideas

  - Reduce cognitive effort

 - Remember key points more easily

## For a 3MT slide, the most useful principles are:

 - Figure–Ground (make the main message stand out)

 - Proximity (group related information)

 - Similarity (consistent colours and fonts)

 - Continuity (guide the audience through the story)

## 1. Figure–Ground Principle

People naturally separate:

The main object (figure)

The background (ground)

## 2. Similarity Principle

- Use consistent colours for related concepts. (Red = Problem
Green = Solution)

- Similarity: Use consistent design elements 


## 3. Continuity Principle

People naturally follow lines, paths, and directions.

Design Tip

Use arrows, pathways, or visual flow to guide attention.

## Suggestions for Improving the Slide


- Remove unnecessary borders and decorative elements.


- Proximity: Group related elements together.

# Presentation Judging Criteria

[https://3mt.ku.edu/3MTRules](https://3mt.ku.edu/3MTRules)

# UQ 3MT competitor guide

[https://threeminutethesis.uq.edu.au/resources/3mt-competitor-guide](https://threeminutethesis.uq.edu.au/resources/3mt-competitor-guide)
